coredata.dc:title: Manipulation of 3D nanocarbon hybrids toward synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots with high photoluminescence quantum yield
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Ning
Tan, Long
Li, Hua-Fei
Hu, Hai-Yan
Wang, Chen
Pan, Ming
Wu, Fan
Wu, Ping
Wang, Xiao-Di
Zeng, Zheling
Deng, Shuguang
Dai, Gui-Ping
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via a top-down approach is still challenging. Herein, we conveniently synthesized nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) via an electrochemical method. In that, the N-containing 3D nanocarbon hybrids were prepared as the carbon and nitrogen sources, which were cut into small fragments in ammonia solution as the electrolyte. Interestingly, N-atoms from the 3D nanocarbon hybrids were successfully retained or converted into other types of N in the obtained GQDs, resulting to a highly doped N content up to 12.3% even after excluding the amino- and pyrrolic N at edges of the N-GQDs. As a matter of fact, the synthesized N-GQDs showed highly crystallized structure, and demonstrated a high photoluminescence quantum yield of ∼19.3%, among the highest values of top-down approach synthesized GQDs. Moreover, it can be also used for adjusting the absorption range of GQDs. Overall, we developed a new strategy to synthesize N-doped GQDs by controlling the carbon source, which opens a new avenue toward achieving other types of heteroatom-doped GQDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reaction yield of N-GQDs and AF-GQDs synthesis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM images of the N-GQDs with different magnifications. (d) Corresponded histogram of N-GQDs. (e) High-resolution TEM image of a single N-GQDs. (f) Corresponding SAED pattern of N-GQDs.

(a) AFM topography image of the N-GQDs deposited on a Si substrate. (b) Height profile extracted along the line in a. (c) Height distribution of N-GQDs. (d) Raman spectra of N-GQDs synthesized by N-CNT/N-Graphene (red curve) and AF-GQDs synthesized by graphite paper (blue curve).
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-GQDs. (b, c) High-resolution XPS of C 1s (b) and N 1s (c) spectra. (d) Scheme of various N-dopants in GQDs. (e) FTIR spectra of N-GQDs and AF-GQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption (red curve). The inset shows the images of the pristine N-GQDs (0.5 mg/mL) aqueous solution and the one under 365 nm UV light. (b) PL 3D mapping of N-GQDs aqueous solution. (c) PL spectra of N-GQDs solution excited at different wavelengths. (d) PL decay curve of N-GQDs (λex = 380 nm). (e) Stability of the N-GQDs under UV light. F and F0 are the PL intensities of pristine N-GQDs and the ones illuminated for certain time. (f) PL spectra of N-GQDs in aqueous solutions at different pH values (λ ex = 380 nm).
Comparison of the composition of N-GQDs and N-CNT/N-Graphene obtained by analyzing the XPS results.

row.entry: Content of different types of N (at %)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of GQDs synthesized by electrochemical method.

head.ce:title: Manipulation of 3D nanocarbon hybrids toward synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots with high photoluminescence quantum yield

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School for Engineering of Matter, Transport and Energy, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ, 85287, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School for Engineering of Matter, Transport and Energy, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ, 85287, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of heteroatom-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via a top-down approach is still challenging. Herein, we conveniently synthesized nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) via an electrochemical method. In that, the N-containing 3D nanocarbon hybrids were prepared as the carbon and nitrogen sources, which were cut into small fragments in ammonia solution as the electrolyte. Interestingly, N-atoms from the 3D nanocarbon hybrids were successfully retained or converted into other types of N in the obtained GQDs, resulting to a highly doped N content up to 12.3% even after excluding the amino- and pyrrolic N at edges of the N-GQDs. As a matter of fact, the synthesized N-GQDs showed highly crystallized structure, and demonstrated a high photoluminescence quantum yield of ∼19.3%, among the highest values of top-down approach synthesized GQDs. Moreover, it can be also used for adjusting the absorption range of GQDs. Overall, we developed a new strategy to synthesize N-doped GQDs by controlling the carbon source, which opens a new avenue toward achieving other types of heteroatom-doped GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the zero-dimensional nanoparticles with size less than 20 nm. They have drawn extensive attention due to the distinct quantum confinement and edge effects [1–3]. With these unique characteristics, GQDs can be applied in various fields, including metal ions detection [4], cellular imaging [5], micro-supercapacitors [6], lithium ion batteries [7] and photocatalysis [8].

In this trend, enormous efforts have been made to develop efficient and controllable approach to fabricate GQDs, which can be mainly divided into the bottom-up and top-down routes [9]. For the bottom-up one, the thermal decomposition [10] and hydrothermal treatment [11,12] were applied to generate GQDs by carbonizing the molecules with aromatic structures [13–15]. Although these methods are convenient to adjust the size distribution of the products [16], the synthetic process is time-consuming, and special precursors or catalysts with critical reaction conditions are needed [15,17,18]. In another vein, top-down approach is performed through cutting various cheap and readily available sp2 carbon materials with large size into small fragments [15,16]. For instance, Pan et al. [19] firstly reported a hydrothermal route, using bulk graphite powder as the raw material, to synthesize blue-luminescent GQDs; Zhu et al. [20] has demonstrated a two-step solvothermal method to cut GO into GQDs in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) reagent. The fluorescence color of the product can be tuned from blue to green with a relative high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 12.2%. Yan et al. [21] used electrochemical method to fabricate green-luminescent GQDs with a uniform size in the range of 3–5 nm. Nevertheless, the GQDs obtained by top-down methods commonly suffer from the drawback of low PLQY [12,22,23].

In this study, we attempted to synthesize nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) with high PLQY via a facile electrochemical method. To the best of our knowledge, although N-doping was commonly reported as an efficient method to increase the PLQY of GQDs in bottom-up routes [11,12], investigation on top-down routes is still limited [24,25]. In our strategy, a technique similar to previously reported electrochemical route was applied for the GQDs synthesis [25]. To do so, the 3D N-doped carbon nanotube/graphene (N-CNT/N-Graphene) hybrids were employed as the carbon materials (see the ESI for details), which can benefit the formation of GQDs in the reaction rather than carbon quantum dots (CQDs). It reveals that N atoms from N-CNT/Graphene were successfully retained in the aromatic rings of the GQDs, leading to a high PLQY as high as 19.3%, among the best values of the GQDs synthesized by top-down routes [26]. Furthermore, the obtained N-GQDs has oxygen-rich and amine function groups at the edges, which allows the N-GQDs to be stably kept in the water for several month without any noticeable precipitation. Our finding opens a new avenue towards synthesizing heteroatom-doped GQDs.

The synthesis of 3D N-CNT/N-Graphene hybrids is based on our previous work [27]. The nickel foam (NF) was first cut into 8 mm × 5 mm and dispersed in acetic acid by ultrasound for about 10 min to clean the surface. Then the NF and melamine were mixed in a mass ratio of 1:5 and placed in a horizontal tube. Hydrogen (99.99%) was introduced as etching agent, with a flow rate of 70 SCCM for about 20 min, before the CVD reactor was heated to 600 °C. When the temperature reached about 800 °C, the sample was annealed for 0.5 h under a mixed gas (flow ratio of Ar:H2 = 5:1) atmosphere. Finally, the product was cooled to the room temperature in Ar atmosphere, and the N-CNT/N-graphene was obtained.

The N-GQDs were prepared by electrochemical method using a two-electrode system, N-CNT/N-graphene (8 mm × 5 mm) as the working electrode and the platinum sheet (15 mm × 15 mm) as counter electrode and 80 mL of ammonia solution (0.2 M) as electrolyte. The time for formation of the N-GQDs is about 4–8 h with a charging current of 0.01 A (Voltage maintained between 5 and 10 V), respectively. The distance between the two electrodes is about 2 cm. After the electrochemical process was completed, the product was collected through a membrane filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm, and purified by a vacuum filter to remove a large portion of unreacted impurity. Finally, the resulting solution was transferred to a rotary evaporator to remove ammonia. In addition, effects of current intensity and concentration of electrolyte on the synthesis of N-GQDs were also investigated (see the ESI for details). The yield of N-GQDs using this unique precursor is ∼82.1%, which is 4 times higher than that using graphite paper as precursor under the similar reaction conditions (2.3).

80 mL of ammonia solution (0.2 M) was used as the electrolyte. The graphite paper (8 mm × 5 mm) was employed as the working electrode, and the platinum sheet was used as counter electrode. The distance between the two electrodes was about 2 cm. The preparation of GQDs was performed on a DC power with a charging current of 0.01 A.

Environmental transmission electron microscopy (ETEM, Titan G2) was used to observe the size and morphology of N-GQDs. Atomic force spectroscopy (AFM, Agilent 5500) were utilized to determine the topographic heights of the N-GQDs. The surface chemistry of N-GQDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi), using a monochromatic Al Kα source. Raman spectra of samples were collected in a black scattering configuration, using a Raman spectroscopy (Raman, Horiba Evolution) with 532 nm excitation source at room temperature. Fluorescence emission spectra and the 3-dimensional Mapping of the N-GQD aqueous dispersion is recorded on a fluorescence emission photometer (FluoroMax-4). The ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of QDs were recorded by an UV–visible spectrometer (Lambda 750) in the range of 200–800 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of QDs were recorded by a FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet 5700).

Quinine sulfate in sulfuric acid solution (0.05 M) was chosen as the standard sample (Фr = 0.54). The quantum yields of N-GQDs in water were calculated according to the following equation:
Φ
qd
=
Φ
r
(
G r a
d
q d
/
G r a
d
r
)
×
(
η
q d
/
η
r
)
2
where Фx is the fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared sample, Grad is the gradient of fluorescence integral intensity versus absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction yield of N-GQDs and AF-GQDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para:
The reaction yields of GQDs synthesis were calculated according to the following equation:
Y i e l d =
(
M
G O D s
/
M
T o t a l
)
× 100 %
Where MGQDs is the mass of the obtained N-GQDs or AF-GQDs, MTotal is the mass of 3D N-CNT/N-Graphene hybrids or graphite paper.

Fig. 1 a–c shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the obtained N-GQDs. The average diameter of the products is 2.0 ± 0.55 nm by analyzing the statistical histogram of the sample as seen in Fig. 1d. The typical high-resolution TEM image of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1e, which exhibits a highly crystallized structure, with the lattice fringes corresponding to the (1120) facet of graphene [28]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1f, which reveals a standard sixfold symmetry of the N-GQDs. All these results demonstrate the high quality of the N-GQDs. The TEM image of amine-group functionalization GQDs (AF-GQDs) has also been obtained for comparison (see Fig. S2a, ESI), and the average diameter is 3.6 ± 1.30 nm with a broader size distribution (Fig. S2b, ESI) compared to the N-GQDs.

The AFM was provided to identify the number of stacked graphene layers in the N-GQDs. Fig. 2 a and b shows the topography image and a typical height profile of the N-GQDs deposited on a Si substrate. We can see that the topography heights of N-GQDs are in the range of 0.5–3 nm. The corresponding histogram of height distribution (Fig. 2c) demonstrates that more than 83% of the N-GQDs have a topography height lower than 2 nm. These results proved that the obtained QDs have 1–3 layered graphene structure, which further confirmed the formation of GQDs rather than CQDs in our synthesis [29]. The detailed structure information of the N-GQDs can be observed from the Raman spectra as seen in Fig. 2d (red curve). Three characteristic peaks located at 1340 cm−1, 1580 cm−1 and 2700 cm−1 have been found, which represents the D band, G band and 2D band of N-GQDs, respectively. The D band is arisen from the breathing mode of sp3 carbon rings and associated with the number of defects in the materials as well [25]. The G band is originated from the bond stretching of sp2 carbon pairs. Therefore, the intensity ratio of ID/IG is generally used to measure the defect and graphitization degree of graphene materials [11]. In our case, a ID/IG value of 0.89 was obtained for the N-GQDs, which is much lower than the AF-GQDs (ID/IG = 1.19, Fig. 2d) synthesized in similar conditions using the graphite paper as the raw material. We noticed that the characteristic Raman spectrum of the N-GQDs is quite similar to the one from N-CNTs/N-Graphene (Fig. S3, ESI). It indicates that the N-GQDs well retained the structure of the raw carbon material.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-GQDs and AF-GQDs are shown in Fig. 3e. The broad absorption bands at 3200-3600 cm−1 are attributed to the N–H and O–H vibrations. These amino and hydroxyl groups at the surface and edges help the GQDs to keep stable in aqueous solution [33]. It is found that the N-GQDs solution can be kept at room temperature for more than half a year without any noticeable precipitation, which has significant meaning for its actual application. The bands at 1634 cm−1, 1278 cm−1 and 998 cm−1 demonstrate the presences of CC, C–C and C–O, respectively, in both samples [21,28]. Importantly, the N-GQDs have a distinguished absorption band at 1384 cm−1, which is arised from the bending vibration of C–N [4]. However, this typical duplet absorption peaks are absent in the spectra of the AF-GQDs. It well demonstrates the existence of N-dopants in the carbon lattice of N-GQDs, in line with the XPS results.

The time-correlated single-photon counting technique was employed to measure the PL lifetime decay of the N-GQDs, and the observed curve can be fit well by a biexponential decay function (Fig. 4d). The average lifetime (τ) of the N-GQDs is ∼5.8 ns calculated from two resolved lifetime components, which is comparable to previous studies [15,25]. The photostability of N-GQDs was tested by placing the N-GQDs solution under high-power UV lamp (365 nm, 500 W) in a distance of 10 cm. After 120 h irradiation, it still has 87% of the initial PL intensity (Fig. 4e). The result is comparable to that of the GQDs reported in ref. [25], indicating that the obtained N-GQDs have good photostability. The effect of pH (from 2 to 10) on PL of N-GQDs has also been investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 4f. The PL peak position was not changed under different pH values. However, the N-GQDs showed better PL intensity in the acidic conditions than the basic ones, and it reached the highest value at pH 2. The trend is similar to the results in ref. [36]. Overall, all results from TEM, Raman and PL characterizations indicate the high quality of our synthesized N-GQDs.

In summary, a facile electrochemical method has been proposed to synthesize monodisperse, highly crystallized and stable blue-luminescent N-GQDs with a PLQY as high as ∼19.3%. The synthesis was processed in aqueous electrolyte without any strong acid treatment. Remarkably, the obtained N-GQDs have a high doped-N content up to ∼12.3%, which is unusual compared to others heteroatoms doped GQDs. This facile, green and economical synthetic method provides a new route for the preparation of hetero-atom doped GQDs, which is promising for the applications of biosensors, photocatalytic and supercapacitors etc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: G.-P.D acknowledges the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 51762032 and 51462022) and the Natural Science Foundation Major Project of Jiangxi Province of China (Grant 20152ACB20012) for financial support of this research. The assistance of Dr. Zhi-Qun Tian (HRTEM measurements) at Guangxi University is also greatly appreciated.
